summary:
The adoption of gunpowder weapons in Europe (c. 1300–1500 AD) was one of the defining military revolutions in Western history, transforming siege warfare, field battles, and ultimately the entire structure of European society by making medieval castles obsolete, reducing the military advantage of armored cavalry, and eventually enabling the small states of Western Europe to project military power globally through the firepower of shipborne cannon. Gunpowder knowledge reached Europe from the Islamic world (itself receiving it via the Mongol networks from China) in the 13th century, with the earliest reliable European cannon references appearing around 1326–1327 in Italian and English sources.

The development of gunpowder weapons proceeded rapidly. Early cannon were large, expensive, and unreliable, but effective enough at the [[event:2986|Battle of Crécy]] (1346) and siege warfare to demonstrate decisive value. The key military transformation was in siegecraft: medieval stone castles, which had been essentially impregnable to earlier siege techniques, were vulnerable to cannon fire that could breach walls in days rather than months. The [[event:2240|fall of Constantinople]] (1453) to Ottoman bronze cannon was the most dramatic demonstration of this revolution. Simultaneously, improvements in iron and bronze founding allowed smaller, more reliable cannon and eventually handheld firearms (arquebus, musket), which began to make the heavily armored mounted knight economically unjustifiable. The 'Military Revolution' initiated by gunpowder required expensive standing armies, specialist gunners and engineers, and state fiscal infrastructure to sustain — accelerating the development of centralized nation-states capable of funding permanent military establishments, contributing to the decline of independent feudal nobility and the rise of absolute monarchy.
